1: The Song of the Sea — the oldest sustained poem in the Bible, the first corporate song of the redeemed community. 'I will sing to YHWH, for he has triumphed gloriously.' A direct connection: Revelation 15:3-4 describes the redeemed who have victory over the beast singing 'the song of God's servant Moses and of the Lamb.' The Song of the Sea is the prototype of all Christian worship — the redeemed singing of what God has done. The NT song adds the Lamb to the divine victory; the Exodus song finds its final form in the song of those delivered through Christ.
2: 'YHWH is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation (yeshuati).' The word for salvation is yeshuah — the same root as Yeshua (Jesus). The confession 'YHWH has become my salvation' is the theological origin of the name Jesus. When Gabriel tells Joseph 'you are to give him the name Jesus, because he will save his people from their sins' (Matthew 1:21), the name carries the entire weight of this confession: YHWH himself has become the salvation, in the person of his Son. The Exodus song declares what the Incarnation enacts.
3: 'YHWH is a warrior; YHWH is his name' — the divine warrior revealed in the Exodus. A revelation of God that culminates in Christ: the warrior who defeated Pharaoh's army in the sea is the warrior who defeats sin, death, and Satan through the cross and resurrection. Revelation 19:11-16 depicts Christ returning as the divine warrior: 'he treads the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty.' The gentle rabbi of Galilee and the divine warrior of the Exodus are the same person — both aspects of the character of the God who fights for his people.
4: Pharaoh's chariots and army cast into the sea — the military power of the world-empire overthrown by YHWH. Theme: the powers and principalities disarmed at the cross (Colossians 2:15). The chariot-force that pursued Israel and was swallowed by the sea is the type of the powers that pursued Christ to the cross and were swallowed by the resurrection. The Exodus song is the first victory hymn; Paul's doxology in 1 Corinthians 15:54-57 ('death has been swallowed up in victory') is its NT form.
5: They sank into the depths like a stone — the completeness of the enemy's destruction. Theme: the finality of Christ's victory. 'The last enemy to be destroyed is death' (1 Corinthians 15:26). The enemies who pursued Israel and sank like stones prefigure the complete and final overthrow of all that opposes the covenant people. The stone imagery recurs: the stone the builders rejected becomes the cornerstone (Psalms 118:22; Matthew 21:42); the grinding stone that falls on those who reject Christ (Matthew 21:44). Stones either sink in the sea of judgment or become the foundation of the new creation.
6: Your right hand, YHWH, was majestic in power; your right hand shattered the enemy — the divine hand of power. The right hand of YHWH extended in the Exodus (Exodus 3:20: 'I will stretch out my hand') is the same right hand extended over the cross. Mark 14:62: Jesus tells the high priest 'you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Mighty One.' The right hand that defeated Pharaoh is the position at which Christ is exalted (Acts 2:33: 'exalted to the right hand of God').
7: In your great majesty you threw down your opponents — the language of holy warfare. Revelation 12:11: 'they triumphed over him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony; they did not love their lives so much as to shrink from death.' The NT's opposition to the enemy is waged by the same divine power that overthrew Egypt — now expressed not in water and fire but in the cross, the testimony of resurrection, and the willingness to follow the Lamb through death to life.
8: By the blast of your nostrils the waters piled up — the breath of YHWH as the instrument of division and creation. Genesis 2:7: YHWH breathed the breath of life into Adam. The same breath-power that created humanity now parts the sea for the redeemed. John 20:22: Jesus breathed on the disciples and said 'receive the Holy Spirit.' The breath/spirit of YHWH that divided the Red Sea becomes the Spirit breathed into the new creation community at Easter. Creation and new creation are both accomplished by the divine breath.
9: The enemy said, 'I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide the plunder' — the boast of the confident pursuer. Theme: the powers that pursue the covenant people always speak the language of certain victory before the divine reversal. The rulers of this age crucified the Lord of glory (1 Corinthians 2:8) — speaking the language of triumph. The empty tomb is the divine reversal that renders the enemy's boast void. 'He said: I will pursue' — but he sank like lead in the mighty waters.
10: You blew with your wind and the sea covered them — the enemy sank like lead. The mighty waters are both the instrument of rescue for Israel and the instrument of judgment for Egypt. Theme: the same event, seen from two sides. The cross is simultaneously the place of Christ's death and the place of death's defeat. The same water that was the Israelites' salvation was the Egyptians' destruction — the atonement is both rescue and judgment, not only mercy but also the vindication of divine justice.
11: 'Who is like you, O YHWH, among the gods? Majestic in holiness, awesome in glorious deeds, doing wonders.' The incomparability hymn — YHWH is without comparison. This forms the theological foundation of the NT's Christological claims: Jesus is identified with YHWH himself, not as a lesser being but as the one who shares the divine incomparability. John 1:1: 'the Word was God.' Philippians 2:6: Jesus was 'in very nature God.' The incomparable YHWH of the Exodus is the incomparable Lord Jesus of the NT.
12: You stretched out your right hand and the earth swallowed them — the divine right hand of judgment. Theme: the same divine power that acts for the delivered acts against those who finally oppose the divine purpose. The Song does not minimize the judgment dimension of the Exodus; it celebrates it. The God who saves is the God who judges; the God who rescues Israel is the God who judges Egypt. The NT holds this together in the cross: the same act that is God's mercy to the condemned is God's judgment on sin and death.
13: 'In your steadfast love (hesed) you have led the people you have redeemed; in your strength you have guided them to your holy dwelling.' The hesed (covenant love/lovingkindness) of YHWH as the engine of redemption and guidance. John 3:16: 'For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son.' Romans 5:8: 'God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.' The hesed that led the Exodus-redeemed to the holy dwelling is the love that leads the new covenant redeemed to the Father's house (John 14:2-3).
14: The nations will hear and tremble — the Exodus event sends reverberations through all the surrounding nations. Theme: the gospel is the power of God to salvation for everyone who believes (Romans 1:16), and the message spreads first to Jews, then to Greeks, then to all nations (Acts 1:8). The trembling of the nations at the Red Sea miracle anticipates the universal scope of the kingdom's advance: 'all the nations you have made shall come and worship before you, O Lord' (Psalms 86:9).
15: The chiefs of Edom, the leaders of Moab, the inhabitants of Canaan — all melt away. Theme: the covenant people's journey toward the inheritance is accompanied by the terrifying reputation that precedes them. Rahab tells the spies: 'a great fear of you has fallen on us, so that all who live in this country are melting in fear because of you' (Joshua 2:9). The same pattern recurs in the spread of the gospel: Acts 19:17: 'When this became known to the Jews and Greeks living in Ephesus, they were all seized with fear.'
16: Terror and dread fell on them; by the greatness of your arm they were as still as a stone — the nations petrified before the advancing covenant people. A revelation of God: the divine presence that goes before the covenant community makes a way through opposition. Theme: Jesus promises that the gates of Hades will not prevail against the church he is building (Matthew 16:18). The stone-still enemies of Israel anticipate the stone rolled away from the tomb — the final immovable object giving way before the power of God.
17: 'You will bring them in and plant them on your own mountain — the place, O Lord, that you made for your dwelling, the sanctuary, O Lord, that your hands established.' The anticipation of the temple as the divine dwelling — the goal toward which the Exodus is headed. A shadow: John 2:19-21 applies temple language to Christ's own body ('the temple he had spoken of was his body'); Revelation 21:22: 'I did not see a temple in the city, because the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple.' The sanctuary established on God's mountain is the goal of redemption — and in Christ, it is both embodied (the Incarnation) and expanded (the new Jerusalem).
18: 'YHWH reigns for ever and ever' — the eternal kingship of YHWH is the culminating declaration of the Song of the Sea. Revelation 11:15: 'The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign for ever and ever.' The eternal reign announced at the Red Sea is the reign proclaimed at the seventh trumpet in Revelation. The Exodus song ends where Revelation ends: the eternal divine kingship established over all opposition. The Song of the Sea is the first movement of a symphony completed in Rev 11 and 19.
19: For the horses of Pharaoh went into the sea with his chariots and riders, and YHWH brought back the waters — the summary statement of what YHWH did. Theme: the gospel is the summary of what God did in Christ: he died, was buried, and was raised on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Both the Exodus and the gospel are narrative summaries of divine action that defines the community's identity. 'Remember the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt' — 'Remember Jesus Christ, raised from the dead, descended from David' (2 Timothy 2:8). The community lives by the summary of what God did.
20: Miriam the prophetess led the women in singing and dancing — the corporate celebration of the redeemed. Theme: the church's worship as the collective response to redemption. Revelation 15:3-4: those who had overcome the beast stood by the sea of glass holding harps and sang the song of Moses the servant of God and of the Lamb. The women dancing with tambourines at the Red Sea anticipates the redeemed multitude singing the combined Song of Moses and of the Lamb in the final scene. All corporate Christian worship carries this structure: victory won, community celebrating, song arising.
21: Miriam's refrain: 'Sing to YHWH, for he has triumphed gloriously; the horse and its rider he has thrown into the sea.' The antiphonal response — the congregation echoes the lead singer. Theme: the structure of Christian worship (call and response, proclamation and affirmation) is established here. The worship of the new Jerusalem is similarly corporate and antiphonal (Revelation 5:9-14 — the four living creatures and the elders, then every creature in heaven and earth). The song that Miriam leads at the Red Sea is the first form of a worship tradition that culminates in the eternal liturgy of the new creation.
22: Three days into the wilderness and no water — the testing immediately follows the triumph. The pattern of the Exodus journey: Passover → Red Sea → three days → Marah. Theme: the wilderness testing follows the salvation event. Jesus is immediately driven into the wilderness after his baptism (Mark 1:12: 'immediately'). The church's wilderness — suffering, testing, apparent drought — follows the Easter event. The three-day motif recurs: three days of no water in the wilderness; three days from the cross to the empty tomb. The pattern of crisis-leading-to-provision is the curriculum of the covenant journey.
23: The waters of Marah were bitter and the people could not drink them — the place of bitter disappointment after the great deliverance. Theme: the reality that Christian experience includes Marah-moments — bitter disappointment after great joy, scarcity after abundance. James 1:2-4: 'consider it pure joy, my brothers and sisters, whenever you face trials of many kinds, because you know that the testing of your faith produces perseverance.' The bitterness of Marah is the precondition for the miracle of sweetness; the testing of faith is the precondition for its maturity.
24: The people grumbled against Moses — the complaint that follows the Marah discovery. Theme: the covenant community's consistent failure to remember the salvation event when facing the next crisis. The same people who sang the Song of the Sea three days earlier now grumble at bitter water. Theme: this is the pattern Jesus addresses in John 6 — the crowd that ate the miraculous loaves and fish (their Red Sea) quickly demands another sign (their Marah). The human memory is short; worship lapses into complaint at the first inconvenience. The wilderness tests what the Red Sea revealed.
25: YHWH showed Moses a piece of wood, which Moses threw into the water and the water became sweet — the wood that transforms bitterness into sweetness. A shadow: this is one of the most evocative types of the cross in the OT. The wood (tree) that transforms bitter water into drinkable water anticipates the tree of the cross that transforms the bitterness of the curse into the sweetness of blessing. Galatians 3:13: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written: cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree.' The wood thrown into Marah is the prototype of the wood of Calvary.
26: 'I am YHWH who heals you' — YHWH Rophe, the divine self-disclosure as healer. A direct revelation of God expressed incarnationally in Christ's healing ministry. Matthew 4:23-24: 'Jesus went throughout Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness among the people.' The God who identifies himself as healer at Marah is the God who heals every disease in human form. The condition placed before the healing (Exodus 15:26a: obey YHWH) mirrors the condition of faith before healing in many of Jesus' miracles — not earning the healing but the posture of trust that receives it.
27: They came to Elim, where there were twelve springs and seventy palm trees, and camped there by the water — the oasis of abundance after the bitterness of Marah. The pattern: Marah (bitterness) → Elim (abundance). Theme: the cross (bitterness, curse, death) leads to resurrection and Pentecost (abundance, Spirit, life). Twelve springs and seventy palms: the numbers of completeness (twelve tribes, seventy nations) encoded into the geography of the provision. The oasis is not the final destination but a place of rest and refreshment in the journey. The new creation will be the ultimate Elim — the place of permanent provision (Revelation 22:1-2: the river of life, the tree of life).
